name: Eye of Ra
description: The Eye of Ra is a powerful symbol and deity in ancient Egyptian mythology, representing the sun god Ra's feminine counterpart and an extension of his power. It embodies both creative and destructive forces, capable of fierce protection and retribution. The Eye is often depicted as a uraeus (cobra), a solar disc, or a human eye, each symbolizing different aspects of its multifaceted nature. It is not a singular object but rather a concept and a divine entity. The Eye's origins are deeply intertwined with Ra's reign and the challenges he faced in maintaining order against chaos. When Ra felt threatened or wronged, the Eye would detach itself and wreak havoc upon his enemies, acting as his divine punisher. However, the Eye also possesses nurturing qualities, representing Ra's care for his creation and his ability to restore balance. The Eye of Ra is crucial in numerous myths, including those involving the goddess Sekhmet, whose bloodlust was eventually calmed by Ra, and the return of the Eye from a distant land, a journey often fraught with peril and symbolic of the sun's daily journey across the sky. Ultimately, the Eye of Ra signifies divine authority, protection, and the cyclical nature of creation and destruction inherent in the cosmos. Its absence would plunge the world into darkness and chaos, while its presence ensures the sun god's continued reign and the preservation of cosmic order. The Eye's return to Ra always signifies a restoration of balance and power.
subtitle: Divine Authority, Protection, Retribution
origin: Emanation of Ra's power and will

keyMyths.title: Sekhmet's Rage
keyMyths.description: When humanity rebelled against Ra, he sent his Eye, in the form of Sekhmet, to punish them. However, Sekhmet's bloodlust became uncontrollable, and Ra had to intervene to prevent the destruction of humanity by tricking her into drinking beer dyed red to resemble blood, calming her rage.
keyMyths.source: The Book of the Heavenly Cow

keyMyths.title: The Return of the Distant Goddess
keyMyths.description: In some versions, the Eye of Ra, personified as a goddess, becomes angry with Ra and leaves him. He sends various deities to retrieve her, facing numerous obstacles and challenges during the journey, symbolizing the sun's journey across the sky and the eventual return of light and order.

sources.author: Geraldine Pinch
sources.work: Egyptian Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Goddesses, and Traditions

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson
